title: Phacoemulsification, pinhole pupilloplasty, and pre-Descemet's endothelial keratoplasty for keratoconus with Fuchs' endothelial dystrophy.
abstract: The comorbidity of keratoconus with Fuchs' endothelial dystrophy with cataract is a rare clinical combination. We present an amalgamation of surgical techniques to manage the above clinical conditions and its complications in single setting. The modified triple procedure, namely, the phacoemulsification, pinhole pupilloplasty, and pre-Descemet's endothelial keratoplasty (PDEK) in the order of description is followed in single sitting. Lens removal by phacoemulsification, correction of irregular astigmatism by pinhole pupilloplasty (pinhole optics), and exchanging the endothelial layer for PDEK forms the main segments of the triple procedure. This combination of techniques may decrease the risk of multiple surgeries and its related complications. Moreover, it will allow the patient for faster visual rehabilitation by improving the uncorrected visual acuity and visual quality.
full_text:
Phacoemulsification, pinhole pupilloplasty, and pre-Descemet’s endothelial keratoplasty for keratoconus with Fuchs’ endothelial dystrophy

The comorbidity of keratoconus with Fuchs’ endothelial dystrophy with cataract is a rare clinical combination. We present an amalgamation of surgical techniques to manage the above clinical conditions and its complications in single setting. The modified triple procedure, namely, the phacoemulsification, pinhole pupilloplasty, and pre-Descemet’s endothelial keratoplasty (PDEK) in the order of description is followed in single sitting. Lens removal by phacoemulsification, correction of irregular astigmatism by pinhole pupilloplasty (pinhole optics), and exchanging the endothelial layer for PDEK forms the main segments of the triple procedure. This combination of techniques may decrease the risk of multiple surgeries and its related complications. Moreover, it will allow the patient for faster visual rehabilitation by improving the uncorrected visual acuity and visual quality.

Keratoconus (KC) is a progressive corneal ectasia, which often is symptomatic at an early age. Ocular allergy, tear film abnormalities, refractive errors, and corneal surface changes are the common associations. Fuchs’ endothelial dystrophy is a rare association with KC. The challenges in the management of cataract in KC with Fuchs’ dystrophy have been widely analyzed, and modifications in surgical maneuvers and intraocular lens (IOL) power calculation have been published. However, there have been only a few reports addressing the treatment of irregular astigmatism by reducing the pupil size. In this surgical technique section, we present a modified triple procedure, namely, the phacoemulsification with IOL implantation, pinhole pupilloplasty (PPP), and pre-Descemet’s endothelial keratoplasty (PDEK) for managing the comorbidity of KC and Fuchs’ dystrophy.

Surgical Technique

Under sterile precautions with peribulbar anesthesia in the operating room, the anterior chamber entry is made with a side port knife and ocular viscoelastic device (OVD) is injected [Video File 1]. Continuous curvilinear capsulorhexis is then performed using cystitome. This is then followed by hydrodissection, hydrodelineation, and phacoemulsification (conventional longitudinal) [Fig. 1a and b]. Irrigation and aspiration are performed, and subsequently, a posterior chamber (PC) IOL is implanted in the capsular bag. A 25 G trans conjunctival trocar anterior chamber maintainer (TACM) is placed at the infero-temporal conjunctiva and single-pass four-throw pupilloplasty (SFT) is performed under direct visualization of pupil center and Purkinje image I intraoperatively [Fig. 1c and d]. Once the pupil is reduced to 1.5 mm size, and it is then confirmed with an intraoperative pupil gauge; the centration is adjusted under the operating microscope with respect to Purkinje image I. The donor graft is then harvested by the regular PDEK technique using pneumatic dissection with the aid of 0.06% trypan blue dye. Recipient’s corneal Descemet’s membrane is stripped by Sinskey’s hook and removed [Fig. 1e]. Subsequently, the donor PDEK graft is loaded into an IOL injector and inserted into the anterior chamber [Fig. 1f]. The graft is centered under saline and unrolled by air [Fig. 1g and h]. The corneal wound is sutured with 10-0 monofilament nylon, and the patient is stabilized in supine position for an hour. Postoperatively, topical antibiotic and steroid combination drops are given and tapered over 4 weeks, while low potency steroid drops are kept till 6 months. Postoperative topography, anterior segment optical coherence tomography (AS OCT), and higher order aberrations are analyzed.

Modified triple procedure for combined keratoconus and Fuchs’ endothelial dystrophy. (a and b) Phacoemulsification with posterior chamber intraocular lens implantation performed as initial step. (c and d) Trocar anterior chamber maintainer placed and single-pass four-throw pupilloplasty performed. The pinhole pupil centered with the reference to corneal Purkinje image 1 from the operating microscope. (e) Recipient bed prepared by removing the Descemet’s membrane under air. (f) Donor graft harvested by pneumatic dissection with staining by trypan blue placed in suitable injector. (g and h) Graft injected under saline and unrolled with air. 10-0 monofilament sutures placed in corneal wound

We have performed the above-described modified triple procedure in both eyes of the same patient with coexisting KC with Fuchs’ endothelial dystrophy. The preoperative unaided distant Snellen visual acuity was 20/80 in both eyes. His unaided vision improved to 20/30 with a 1.5 mm sized pinhole template during the preoperative assessment. The preoperative manifest refraction was -1 D sphere with -1.5 D Cylinder @ 90 (OD) and -1 D and -1.5 D Cylinder @140 degrees (OS), respectively. The preoperative topography showed moderate KC [Fig. 2a and c] with bilateral Vogt’s striae with diffuse corneal haze, Fleischer rings, and an inferior corneal cone. His specular microscopy showed endothelial guttate in both eyes [Fig. 2b and d], and he had a specular count of 928 cells/sq mm and 528 cells/sq mm in OD and OS, respectively. Postoperatively, the unaided vision improved to 20/30 in both eyes. Slit-lamp biomicroscopy revealed a pinhole pupil with a clear cornea with good endothelial function [Fig. 2f, h and Fig. 3a-d]. There was a mild change in root mean square (RMS) values and point spread function [Fig. 4a-d]. The preoperative pupil size of 3.8 mm in both eyes was reduced to 1.3 mm and 1.5 mm in OD and OS, respectively. The preoperative chord μ reduced from 0.5 mm to 0.3 mm. There was reduction in vertical coma (preoperative -1.6 μm to postoperative -1.1 μm) and higher-order RMS (preoperative 2 μm to postoperative 1.5 μm). The postoperative central corneal thickness was 531 μm and 518 μm in OD and OS, respectively, with a well-apposed graft in situ [Fig. 3]. There was no significant difference in preoperative and postoperative equivalent keratometry reading (EKR) (p = 0.655) [Fig. 2e, 2g]. Subjectively, the patient was satisfied with the visual outcome. The patient was followed up for the next 6 months with stable visual acuity.

Preoperative sagittal curvature map showing moderated keratoconus with inferior cone and preoperative endothelial cell layer showing guttate changes in OD (a and b) and OS (c-d), Postoperative sagittal curvature map and endothelial layer in OD (e and f) and postoperative sagittal curvature map and endothelial layer in OS (g and h)

Postoperative clinical photograph of OD (a) and OS (b) and anterior segment optical coherence tomography of (c) OD and (d) OS showing postoperative pupil and well-apposed graft

Preoperative (a and b) and postoperative (c and d) change in point spread function and Strehl ratio of eyes which underwent modified triple procedure. (a and c) OD, (b and c) OS

Discussion

Keratoconus is a known corneal ectasia with various corneal associations, including Fuchs’ endothelial dystrophy. The challenge in this comorbidity is the thinning of KC and masked edema of corneal decompensation, which can delay the early diagnosis. However, the advancement in technology has made early diagnosis possible in both the conditions. Although there have been reports on the combined management of Fuchs’ endothelial dystrophy with KC like timely cataract surgery with proper IOL power calculation or combining with endothelial keratoplasty, there have been no reports on the management of irregular astigmatism using the pinhole optics principle and PDEK procedure. In this technique section, we highlight the importance and efficiency of successful management of the comorbid condition with a modified triple procedure.

Concurrent KC with Fuchs’ endothelial dystrophy has been managed by conventional phacoemulsification followed later by penetrating keratoplasty. Vira et al. noted that topographically, the characteristic inferior steepening of KC did not change after Descemet stripping endothelial keratoplasty (DSEK); however, flatter postoperative keratometry measurements were achieved. They reported that if a DSEK is performed for Fuchs’ dystrophy before apical corneal scarring from KC, a good visual outcome may be achieved. Though KC is a primary posterior corneal disease, no difficulty in graft adherence was noted after the endothelial keratoplasty procedure.

Higher-order aberrations that induced visual quality disturbances are known in KC. If combined endothelial edema is encountered, the visual quality will be significantly affected. Toric intraocular lens is an option in eyes with stable KC with cataract. Targeting postoperative one diopter myopia, combining limbal relaxing incisions, and placing hydrophobic acrylic lenses have been tried in combined cataract and Fuchs’ dystrophy eyes for future endothelial keratoplasty. The implantation of a segmented IOL using the ray tracing method and Fourier optics can compensate for the overall coma, improving the quality of vision compared with a spherical monofocal IOL due to lower displacements of the retinal image or tilt. However, the challenges in visual rehabilitation, cost affordability, and time constraints are unavoidable in staged procedures.

In pinhole optics, there have been prior reports on pinhole pupilloplasty showing satisfactory results in eyes with irregular astigmatism. The technique of combining pinhole pupilloplasty helps filter the stray light from the periphery of the cornea in cases with higher-order corneal aberrations. The pinhole blocks distorted and unfocused rays and isolates more focused central and paracentral rays through the central aperture, thereby reducing aberrations of the optical system. It is known that ocular aberrations are directly proportional to the pupil diameter. McCormick et al. showed that a pupil of 6 mm in a normal population with virgin cornea would have an HRMS of 0.38 ± 0.13 μm and also reported that corneal surgery induced changes in symptomatic patients can be as high as 1.31 ± 0.58 μm. Patients with more than 0.75 μm of HRMS in 6 mm pupil can be symptomatic. Pinhole optics have the advantage of reducing the pupil size-related aberrations with an extended depth of focus with good quality of vision. Trindade et al. have used pinhole optic device in the ciliary sulcus in a piggyback configuration to minimize the effect of corneal aberrations. Agarwal et al. reported the use of an Extra focus pinhole IOL, which addresses both irregular astigmatism and pupillary defects. Thus, pinhole IOL can be another alternative in such conditions; however, specialized IOL has to be procured preoperatively.

The limitation one may think of for PPP is the difficult fundus examination and abnormal cosmetic pupil shape. After SFT, with strong cycloplegics, central fundus can be seen and minimal pupil dilation can always be obtained. If there were a need for pupil dilation for vitreo-retinal surgical indications, pupilloplasty can be reversed at anytime. Inflammation from tissue manipulation has been negligible as no significant uveitis or macular edema was noted in eyes with SFT. Independence from contact lenses and spectacles has been achieved with good satisfaction in KC patients by the modified triple procedure. Nevertheless, long-term effects, night vision changes, and surgical learning curve are to be analyzed in future. To conclude, we believe that the technique has been promising in eyes with concurrent KC and Fuchs’ endothelial dystrophy with irregular astigmatism.
